name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا لَقِيتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ زَحْفًا فَلَا تُوَلُّوهُمُ ٱلْأَدْبَارَ (١٥)
```

```english
O you who have faith! When you encounter the faithless advancing [for battle], do not turn your backs [to flee] from them. (15)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُۥٓ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفًا لِّقِتَالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزًا إِلَىٰ فِئَةٍ فَقَدْ بَآءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٦)
```

```english
Whoever turns his back [to flee] from them that day—unless [he is] diverting to fight or retiring towards another troop—shall certainly earn Allah’s wrath, and his refuge shall be hell, an evil destination. (16)
```

```arabic
فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَتَلَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَمَىٰ ۚ وَلِيُبْلِىَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْهُ بَلَآءً حَسَنًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٧)
```

```english
You did not kill them; rather, it was Allah who killed them; and you did not throw when you threw, rather, it was Allah who threw, that He might test the faithful with a good test from Himself. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (17)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُوهِنُ كَيْدِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٨)
```

```english
Such is the case, and [know] that Allah undermines the stratagems of the faithless. (18)
```

```arabic
إِن تَسْتَفْتِحُوا۟ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْفَتْحُ ۖ وَإِن تَنتَهُوا۟ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تَعُودُوا۟ نَعُدْ وَلَن تُغْنِىَ عَنكُمْ فِئَتُكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَوْ كَثُرَتْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٩)
```

```english
If you sought a verdict, the verdict has certainly come to you; and if you cease [your belligerence against the Prophet and his followers], it is better for you, but if you resume, We [too] shall return and your troops will never avail you though they should be ever so many, and [know] that Allah is with the faithful. (19)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَلَا تَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَنتُمْ تَسْمَعُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Obey Allah and His Apostle, and do not turn away from him while you hear [him]. (20)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
Do not be like those who say, ‘We hear,’ though they do not hear. (21)
```

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ شَرَّ ٱلدَّوَآبِّ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلصُّمُّ ٱلْبُكْمُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight are the deaf and dumb who do not exercise their reason. (22)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِمْ خَيْرًا لَّأَسْمَعَهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ أَسْمَعَهُمْ لَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
Had Allah known any good in them, He would have surely made them hear, and were He to make them hear, surely they would turn away, being disregardful. (23)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ ۖ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ ٱلْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِۦ وَأَنَّهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life. Know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart and that toward Him you will be mustered. (24)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ فِتْنَةً لَّا تُصِيبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنكُمْ خَآصَّةً ۖ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢٥)
```

```english
And beware of a punishment, which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively, and know that Allah is severe in retribution. (25)
```

```arabic
وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ أَنتُمْ قَلِيلٌ مُّسْتَضْعَفُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ تَخَافُونَ أَن يَتَخَطَّفَكُمُ ٱلنَّاسُ فَـَٔاوَىٰكُمْ وَأَيَّدَكُم بِنَصْرِهِۦ وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
Remember when you were few, abased in the land, and feared lest the people should despoil you, and He gave you refuge, and strengthened you with His help, and provided you with all the good things so that you may give thanks. (26)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَخُونُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوٓا۟ أَمَـٰنَـٰتِكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts knowingly. (27)
```

```arabic
وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٨)
```

```english
Know that your possessions and children are only a test, and that Allah—with Him is a great reward. (28)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تَتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّكُمْ فُرْقَانًا وَيُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢٩)
```

```english
O you who have faith! If you are wary of Allah, He shall appoint a criterion for you, and absolve you of your misdeeds, and forgive you, for Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (29)
```

## (Commentary)

These are commands and prohibitions related to Islamic struggle (الجهاد) — *the striving in the way of Allah* — suited to the flow of the narrative. They include an exhortation toward godwariness (تقوى الله) — *the inner state of being conscious of Allah’s presence and guarding oneself against His displeasure* — and a warning and intimidation against opposing Allah and His Messenger or being exposed to His wrath, the Glorified. Within them is an allusion to some of what occurred during the Battle of Badr regarding the favors (منن) and graces (أيادي) of Allah upon the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا زَحْفاً فَلا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبارَ** — "O you who have faith! When you encounter the faithless advancing [for battle], do not turn your backs to them." (8:15). The word "encounter" (اللقاء) is a verbal noun from the triliteral root *laqiya yalqa* (لقي يلقى) and the augmented form *laqa yulaqi* (لاقى يلاقي). Al-Raghib said in *Mufradat al-Quran*: "Encounter (اللقاء) is the meeting of a thing and its coincidence together. Sometimes it is used to express either one of them. It is said: he met him (لقيه يلقاه) with a meeting (لقاء), a joining (لقيا), and an encounter (لقية). This is said regarding perception by the senses, by sight, and by insight (البصيرة)." He cited: **لَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ الْمَوْتَ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَلْقَوْهُ** — "You certainly used to long for death before you encountered it" (3:143), and: **لَقَدْ لَقِينا مِنْ سَفَرِنا هذا نَصَباً** — "We have certainly encountered much fatigue on this journey of ours" (18:62). The "meeting with Allah" (ملاقاة الله) is an expression for the Resurrection (القيامة) and the return to Him. He said: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ مُلاقُوهُ** — "and know that you will encounter Him" (2:223), and: **الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا اللهِ** — "those who are certain that they will encounter Allah" (2:249). The word *al-liqa* (اللقاء) means *al-mulaqah* (الملاقاة). He said: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا** — "And those who do not expect to encounter Us say..." (25:21), and: **إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ** — "...toward your Lord, and you will encounter Him" (84:6). End quote.

It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Encounter (اللقاء) is a gathering in the manner of proximity (المقاربة), for a gathering might occur without proximity and thus not be an encounter, such as the gathering of accidents (الأعراض) in a single locus (المحل)." End quote.

It is also stated therein: "Advancing (الزحف) is to draw near little by little, and mutual advancing (التزاحف) is mutual drawing near. It is said: he advanced (زحف يزحف زحفا), and 'I advanced toward the people' (أزحفت للقوم) when you drew near to fight them and stood firm against them." Al-Layth said: "The *zahf* (الزحف) is a group advancing toward an enemy of theirs all at once; its plural is *zuhuf* (زحوف)." End quote.

"Turning the backs" (تولية الأدبار) to the enemies means making the backs face them, which is to turn one's back to the enemy and face the direction of retreat.

The address of the verse is general, not specific to a particular time or a specific battle. Thus, there is no basis for restricting it to the Battle of Badr or limiting the prohibition of fleeing from the battlefield to it, as is reported from some exegetes. Furthermore, you have already realized that the apparent context of the verses indicates they were revealed after the Battle of Badr, not on the day itself, and that these verses are a continuation of what was at the beginning of the Surah in His saying: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ** — "They ask you about the spoils. Say, ‘The spoils belong to Allah and the Apostle.’" (8:1). There is a completion to this discussion that will reach you in the "Discussion of Narrations," Allah the Exalted willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ** — "Whoever turns his back [to flee] from them that day—unless it be for a tactical maneuver or to join another company..." (8:16) to the end of the verse. "Tactical maneuver" (التحرف) is to move away from the straight direction toward the "edge" (الحرف), which is the side of a thing. This is when the fighter swerves or turns from one direction to another to gain an advantage over his enemy or to initiate a stratagem against him. "Joining" (التحيز) is to take a "space" (الحيز), which is a place. A "company" (الفئة) is a segment of a group of people. Joining a company means the fighter turns away from facing the enemy alone to join a group of his people to attach himself to them and fight alongside them.

"Returning" (البواء) is to return to a place and settle in it. For this reason, Al-Raghib said: "The root of *al-bawa* (البواء) is the equality of parts in a place, the opposite of *al-nabwah* (النبوة), which is the incompatibility of parts." End quote. Thus, the meaning of His saying: **باءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللهِ** — "he shall certainly incur Allah’s wrath" (8:16) is that he has returned while accompanied by the wrath of Allah.

The meaning of the two verses is: O you who believe, when you encounter those who disbelieve in an advancing meeting or while you are advancing for combat, do not flee from them. Whoever flees from them on that day—meaning at that time—has returned with the wrath of Allah upon him, and his abode is Hell, and an evil destination it is, unless his fleeing is for a tactical maneuver for combat or to join another company, in which case there is no harm in it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ قَتَلَهُمْ وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ رَمى** — "You did not kill them; rather it was Allah who killed them; and you did not throw when you threw, rather it was Allah who threw..." (8:17) to the end of the verse. Contemplation of the context leaves no doubt that the verse refers to the Battle of Badr and what the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did by throwing a handful of pebbles at them, and what the believers did by putting them to the sword and inflicting a heavy slaughter upon them. The end of the verse, namely His saying: **وَلِيُبْلِيَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْهُ بَلاءً حَسَناً** — "and that He might test the faithful with a good test from Himself" (8:17), indicates that the discourse is proceeding in the manner of a favor from Him, the Exalted. Allah has affirmed the very thing He negated in a single sentence, namely His saying: **وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ** — "and you did not throw when you threw."

From all these evidences, it is concluded that the meaning of His saying: **فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ قَتَلَهُمْ وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ رَمى** is the negation that the Battle of Badr—and the eradication of the polytheists, the triumph over them, and the victory achieved therein—proceeded according to the usual course and the known laws of nature (نواميس الطبيعة). How could it be possible for a group who were a small band—among whom there was, according to what is narrated, only one or two horses, a few coats of mail, and a few swords—to eradicate an army equipped with horses, weapons, men, provisions, and mounts, who were many times their number and incomparable to them in strength and intensity, possessing the means of victory, the factors of prowess, and the appropriate position for advancement?

However, Allah, the Glorified, through the angels He sent down, made the feet of the believers firm and cast terror into the hearts of the polytheists. He brought about defeat through the pebbles the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) threw at them, such that the believers overwhelmed them with killing and capture. Thereby, their plot was nullified, their breaths were extinguished, and their clamor was silenced.

It is therefore appropriate to attribute the killing that befell them at the hands of the believers, and the throwing that scattered their ranks and cast defeat upon them, to Him, the Glorified, rather than to the believers.

Thus, the negation in the verse proceeds as a claim made with a kind of metaphorical nuance (عناية), considering the attribution of the killing in its various aspects to a divine, non-ordinary cause. This does not contradict its attribution—in terms of the events that occurred—to their immediate, customary causes in nature, such that the believers are considered the killers of those they slew, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) the thrower of the pebbles he threw.

Regarding His saying: **وَلِيُبْلِيَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْهُ بَلاءً حَسَناً**, it appears that the pronoun in "from Him" (مِنْهُ) refers to Allah the Exalted. The sentence is to explain the purpose (الغاية) and is conjoined to an elided, estimated phrase. The estimation is: Allah only did what He did of killing them and throwing at them for great interests He possesses, and that He might test (ليبلي) the believers and try them with a good trial (بلاء) and testing, or that He might bestow upon them a good favor (نعمة), which is the annihilation of their opponent, the elevation of the word of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — through them, and enriching them with the spoils they gained.

His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** — "Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing" (8:17) is a justification for His saying: **وَلِيُبْلِيَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ**, meaning that He, the Exalted, tests them because He is all-hearing of their plea for help and all-knowing of their state; thus He tests them with a good test from Himself.

The derivation at the beginning of the verse: **فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ** etc., is connected to the meaning contained in the preceding verses: **إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ** — "When you were pleading with your Lord for help..." (8:9) to the end of the verses. For they enumerate the favors of Allah upon them, such as sending down the angels, aiding them with them, covering them with slumber, sending rain upon them from the sky, and what was revealed to the angels to support them, make their feet firm, and cast terror into the hearts of their enemies. When the discourse reached this point, He derived from it His saying: **فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ قَتَلَهُمْ وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ رَمى**.

Based on this, His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمُ** to His saying: **وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ** is a parenthetical statement (معترضة) connected to His saying: **فَاضْرِبُوا فَوْقَ الْأَعْناقِ وَاضْرِبُوا مِنْهُمْ كُلَّ بَنانٍ** — "so strike their necks and strike all their fingertips" (8:12), or to the meaning understood from the narrated sentences. His saying: **فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ** etc., is connected to what precedes it in terms of structural order (النظم).

Two other views are sometimes mentioned regarding the order of the verse:

First: That when Allah, the Glorified, commanded them to kill in the preceding verse, He mentioned immediately after it that the victory on the day of Badr and the subduing of the polytheists were only through His help and assistance, as a reminder of the favor. This was mentioned by Abu Muslim.

Second: That when they were commanded to fight, and then some of them began saying, "I killed so-and-so" and "I did such-and-such," the verse was revealed as a warning to them so they would not be conceited by their deeds. It is also said that the "fa" (ف) in His saying: **فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ** is merely to link the sentences to one another. The correct view is what we presented first.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلِكُمْ وَأَنَّ اللهَ مُوهِنُ كَيْدِ الْكافِرِينَ** — "That [is your prize], and [know] that Allah saps the stratagems of the faithless." (8:18). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "**ذلِكُمْ** (That) is in the nominative position, and likewise **أَنَّ اللهَ** (that Allah) is in the nominative position. The estimation is: 'The matter is that' and 'The matter is that Allah is the sapper.' Similarly, the case for what preceded in His saying: **ذلِكُمْ فَذُوقُوهُ وَأَنَّ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابَ النَّارِ** — 'That [is your prize]; so taste it, and [know] that for the faithless is the punishment of the Fire.' (8:14). Whoever says that **ذلِكُمْ** is a subject (مبتدأ) and **فَذُوقُوهُ** is its predicate (خبر) has erred, because what follows the 'fa' cannot be a predicate for a subject. It is not permissible to say: 'Zayd so is departing' (زيد فمنطلق), nor 'Zayd so strike him' (زيد فاضربه) unless you imply 'this' (هذا), intending: 'This is Zayd, so strike him.'" End quote. Thus, the meaning of the verse is: The matter is that which We have mentioned, and the matter is that Allah saps the stratagem of the disbelievers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ تَسْتَفْتِحُوا فَقَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْفَتْحُ** — "If you seek a verdict, the verdict has certainly come to you..." (8:19) to the end of the verse. The apparent sense of the verse, given the sequence of sentences it contains, such as His saying: **وَإِنْ تَنْتَهُوا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ** — "and if you give up, it will be better for you" (8:19) and His saying: **وَإِنْ تَعُودُوا نَعُدْ** — "and if you return, We [too] shall return" (8:19) etc., is that the address therein is to the polytheists rather than the believers, by way of a shift (التفات) for sarcasm (التهكم). This is consistent with His saying in the previous verse: **وَأَنَّ اللهَ مُوهِنُ كَيْدِ الْكافِرِينَ**.

The meaning is: If you sought a verdict and asked Allah, O polytheists, to decide between you and the believers, then the verdict has come to you through what Allah manifested of the truth on the day of Badr, such that the turn of fortune was for the believers against you. If you cease your plotting against Allah and His Messenger, it is better for you; and if you return to the like of your plotting, We shall return to the like of how We sapped your plot. Your company shall not avail you anything, even if it be numerous, just as it did not avail this time; and indeed, Allah is with the believers, and whoever He is with shall never be overcome.

This is supported by what is reported that Abu Jahl said on the day of Badr when the two groups lined up or when the two parties met: "O Allah, Muhammad has severed our ties of kinship and brought us that which we do not recognize, so grant victory against him." In some narrations—which is more appropriate—as in *al-Majma‘* from Abu Hamzah: Abu Jahl said: "O Allah, our Lord, our ancient religion and the new religion of Muhammad—whichever of the two religions is more beloved to You and more pleasing to You, then grant victory to its people today."

Some have mentioned that the address in the verse is to the believers, and they have interpreted the contents of its sentences in a way that sound taste (الذوق السليم) does not accept. There is no benefit in prolonging by mentioning them and discussing them; whoever desires that should consult the lengthy works.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَلا تَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ وَأَنْتُمْ تَسْمَعُونَ** — "O you who have faith! Obey Allah and His Apostle, and do not turn away from him while you hear." (8:20). The pronoun, according to what the context provides, refers to the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The meaning is: Do not turn away from the Messenger while you hear what he delivers to you of the true call and what he commands you and forbids you of that which contains the welfare of your religion and your world. The focus of the discourse is his military commands, even if its wording is more general.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ قالُوا سَمِعْنا وَهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُونَ** — "Do not be like those who say, ‘We hear,’ though they do not hear." (8:21). The meaning is clear, and it contains a kind of allusion (تعريض) to the polytheists, as they said, "We hear," while they do not hear. Allah has narrated this from them when He said after several verses: **وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا قالُوا قَدْ سَمِعْنا لَوْ نَشاءُ لَقُلْنا مِثْلَ هذا** — "When Our signs are recited to them, they say, ‘We have heard [this] already. If we wanted, we could say the like of it.’" (8:31). However, they lied and did not hear; had they heard, they would have responded, as Allah the Exalted said: **وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها** — "they have ears with which they do not hear" (7:179). And the Exalted said, narrating from the inmates of the Blaze: **وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ** — "And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze.’" (67:10). Thus, what is meant by "hearing" in the first verse is receiving the true speech, which is a sound through the ear; and in the second verse, it is submission (الانقياد) to what the heard true speech contains.

The two verses—as you see—are an address related to the believers, connected in a way to the verse preceding them, and an allusion to the polytheists. For when He turned to the polytheists, censuring them and mocking them for their request for a verdict, and mentioned to them that victory is always for the word of faith over the word of disbelief and for the call of truth over the call of falsehood, He turned to His party, who are the believers, commanding them to obey Him and His Messenger, and warning them against turning away from him after hearing the word of truth, and against being like those who said, "We hear," while they do not hear.

It is possible that the verse contains an allusion to a number of the people of Mecca who believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), but when their hearts were not purified from doubt, they went out with the polytheists to Badr to fight the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and thus were afflicted with what the polytheists of Quraysh were afflicted with. It has been reported in the news: that a group from Quraysh accepted Islam in Mecca and their fathers detained them, so they went out with Quraysh on the day of Badr. They were Qays ibn al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, Ali ibn Umayyah ibn Khalaf, al-As ibn Munabbih ibn al-Hajjaj, al-Harith ibn Zam‘ah, and Qays ibn al-Fakih ibn al-Mughirah. When they saw the small number of Muslims, they said: "These poor people, their religion has deluded them." Allah will mention them after several verses in His saying: **إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ** — "When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness were saying, ‘Their religion has deluded them.’" (8:49).

It is sometimes said that those meant by "those who say, ‘We hear,’ though they do not hear" are the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) from the Jews of Qurayzah and al-Nadir. This is far-fetched.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللهِ الصُّمُّ الْبُكْمُ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْقِلُونَ** — "Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight are the deaf and the dumb who do not exercise their reason." (8:22) to the end of the two verses. This is an allusion and censure for those previously mentioned among the disbelievers, as indicated by the context of the speech and the relative pronouns and pronouns used for rational beings (أولي العقل) contained in the verse. Accordingly, it appears that the definite article "al" (ال) in "the deaf and the dumb" (الصُّمُّ الْبُكْمُ) is for specific reference (العهد الذكري). The meaning boils down to the fact that the worst of all that crawls upon the earth among the genera and species of animals are these deaf and dumb ones who do not reason. They do not reason only because they have no way to receive the truth due to their lack of hearing and speech; thus they do not hear and do not speak.

Then Allah the Exalted mentioned that He only afflicted them with deafness and dumbness—such that they do not hear the word of truth nor speak the word of truth, and in short, He deprived them of the blessing of hearing and acceptance—because He, the Exalted, did not find any good (خيرا) in them and did not know of it. Had there been any, He would have known it. But He did not know it, so He did not grant them the success (التوفيق) to hear and accept. Even if He, the Exalted, had granted them hearing in this state, hearing and acceptance would not have remained firm in them; rather, they would have turned away from the truth while being averse.

From here it is known that what is meant by "good" (الخير) is the goodness of the inner secret (حسن السريرة) by which the readiness (الاستعداد) to accept the truth is established and settled in the heart. And what is meant by His saying: **وَلَوْ أَسْمَعَهُمْ** — "Had He made them hear" (8:23) is making them hear on the assumption of the absence of established, settled readiness. Understand this, so that it is not objected that if Allah had made them hear and granted them acceptance of the truth, it would necessitate the realization of good in them, and there would be no reason for their turning away and aversion. This is because the condition in His saying: "Had He made them hear" is based on the assumption of their lacking good, as the context provides.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** — "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life." (8:24). Since He called them in His saying: "Obey Allah and His Apostle" etc., to obey the true call and not to turn away from it after hearing it, He emphasized it a second time by calling for the response to Allah and the Messenger in the Messenger's call, by explaining the reality of the matter and the actual pillar upon which this call relies. This is that this call is a call to that which gives life to the human being by bringing him out from the place of annihilation and ruin. His position in existence is that Allah, the Glorified, is closer to him than his heart and that he will be mustered to Him; so let him take heed, gather his concern, and resolve his determination.

Life (الحياة) is the most delightful blessing and the highest commodity that a living being believes for itself. How could it not be? For it sees nothing beyond it but non-existence and nullity. Its effect, which is consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة), is that for which life is sought and in which the human being finds comfort. He never ceases to flee from ignorance and the loss of freedom of will and choice. The human being—who is one of the living beings—has been equipped with that which preserves his spiritual life, which is the reality of his existence, just as every species of creation has been equipped with that which preserves its existence and survival.

This human apparatus identifies for him his goods and benefits, and warns him against the places of evil and harm.

Since this divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — which drives the human species toward its type of happiness and good and invites it toward the benefits of its existence, is a guidance according to the creative command (التكوين) and in the mode of creation, and since it is impossible for an error to occur in the creative command, it was an absolute necessity that the human being perceive the happiness of his existence with a perception in which no doubt occurs. Just as other created species move toward what contains the good of their existence and the benefits of their person without erring therein from the perspective of their primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. Confusion only occurs in what occurs due to the influence of other opposing factors and causes that exert a contrary effect on him, whereby the thing swerves from what is good for it to what is evil, and from what contains its benefit to what contains harm returning to it. This is like a heavy earthly body that, according to its earthly nature, settles upon the surface of the earth; then it moves away from the earth by movement toward the height by the push of a pusher that compels it contrary to its nature. When the effect of the push ceases, it returns to its resting place by moving toward the earth in a straight line, unless a preventer prevents it and takes it out of the straight path into swerving and crookedness.

This is what the Noble Quran insists upon: that the human being is not unaware of what contains his happiness in life in terms of knowledge and action, and that he perceives through his primordial nature (الفطرة) what is the truth of belief and action. The Exalted said: **فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** — "the nature [fashioned] by Allah, in which He has fashioned mankind. There is no changing Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion" (30:30). And the Exalted said: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى** — "who created and fashioned, who determined and guided" (87:2-3) until He said: **فَذَكِّرْ إِنْ نَفَعَتِ الذِّكْرى سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَنْ يَخْشى وَيَتَجَنَّبُهَا الْأَسْقَى** — "So admonish, for admonition is indeed beneficial. He who fears will take admonition, and the most wretched will shun it" (87:9-11). And the Exalted said: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها** — "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, and one who betrays it is certainly a failure" (91:7-10).

Yes, the human being might sometimes miss the path of truth in belief or action and stumble in his gait, but not because the human primordial nature and divine guidance have cast him into misguidance and brought him to destruction. Rather, it is because he neglected his intellect, forgot his right guidance, and followed the desire of his soul and what the hosts of the devils beautified in his eyes. The Exalted said: **إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَما تَهْوَى الْأَنْفُسُ وَلَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمُ الْهُدى** — "They follow nothing but conjectures and the desires of the [lower] souls, while there has already come to them guidance from their Lord" (53:23). And He said: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ** — "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly" (45:23).

So these matters to which the human primordial nature invites—of true knowledge and action—are the concomitants of the happy human life. This is the real life (الحياة الحقيقية) which is worthy of being exclusively called "life." The happy life follows it, just as it necessitates life, follows it, and returns it to its place if life weakens in its place due to the arrival of what opposes it and nullifies the rightness of its action.

If the human being swerves from the even path (سوي الصراط) to which the human primordial nature guides him and toward which divine guidance drives him, he has lost the concomitants of the happy life—of beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds (الصالحات) — *the deeds that are in accordance with the divine command and lead to spiritual perfection* — and has joined the dead through the descent of ignorance and the corruption of free will and beneficial action. Nothing gives him life except true knowledge and true action, which are what the primordial nature (الفطرة) calls for. This is what is alluded to by the verse we are discussing: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ**.

The "lam" (ل) in His saying: **لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** carries the meaning of "to" (إلى), which is common in usage. That to which the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) calls is the true religion (الدين الحق), which is Islam, which the Noble Quran explains as the following of the primordial nature (الفطرة) in what it calls for of beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds.

Life, according to what the Noble Quran sees, has another meaning more subtle than what we see according to a superficial, naive view. For we only know of life, at first glance, that by which the human being lives in his worldly emergence until death befalls him. It is that which accompanies consciousness and volitional action, and the like of it or something close to it exists in non-humans as well among other animal species. But Allah, the Glorified, says: **وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known!" (29:64). This implies that the human being, while enjoying this life, is occupied with nothing but illusions, and that he is occupied by it away from what is more important and more obligatory of the goals of his existence and the purposes of his spirit. Thus, he is in a veil (حجاب) cast over him that separates him from the reality of what he seeks and desires of life.

This is what is alluded to by His saying, the Exalted—and it is among the addresses of the Day of Resurrection: **لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ** — "‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today.’" (50:22).

Thus, for the human being, there is another life higher in rank and more precious in value than this worldly life which Allah, the Glorified, considers play and diversion. It is the life of the Hereafter (الحياة الأخروية) whose veil will be removed from its face. It is the life that is not mixed with play and diversion, nor do vanity and sin approach it. The human being does not walk therein except by the light of faith and the spirit of servitude (العبودية). The Exalted said: **أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ** — "He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him" (58:22). And the Exalted said: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها** — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122).

So this is another life, loftier in stature and higher in station than the general worldly life in which the mute animal might share with the human being. It appears from the likes of His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ** — "and strengthened him with the Holy Spirit" (2:253) and His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا** — "Thus have We revealed to you a spirit of Our behest" (42:52) that there is another life above these two mentioned lives, the discussion of which will reach you in its appropriate place, Allah willing.

In short, for the human being, there is a real life (حياة حقيقية) more noble and more perfect than his worldly religious life, which he dons when his readiness is completed by adorning himself with the ornament of religion and entering the group of the righteous friends (الأولياء الصالحين), just as he donned the worldly life when his readiness to don it was completed while he was a human embryo.

Upon this applies His saying, the Exalted, in the verse under discussion: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ**. For donning that to which the true call of Islam invites draws the human being to this real life, just as this life is a spring from which Islam gushes and from which beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds arise. In the meaning of this verse is His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهو مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do." (16:97).

The verse—namely His saying therein: **إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** — is absolute (مطلق), not refusing to include all of his call (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that gives life to the hearts, or some of it which possesses the nature of giving life, or its results which are the types of real happy life, such as the happy life in the proximity of Allah, the Glorified, in the Hereafter.

From here it appears that there is no basis for restricting the verse to what most exegetes have restricted it to. Some said: The meaning of His saying: "when he summons you to that which will give you life," considering the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول), is: when he calls you to struggle (الجهاد), for in it is the revival of your affair and the honoring of your religion.

It was said: The meaning is when he calls you to martyrdom (الشهادة) in the way of Allah in the struggle against your enemy, for Allah, the Glorified, considered the martyrs alive, as in His saying: **وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتاً بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمُ يُرْزَقُونَ** — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord" (3:169).

It was said: The meaning is when he calls you to faith (الإيمان), for it is the life of the heart and disbelief is its death; or when he calls you to the truth.

It was said: The meaning is when he calls you to the Quran and knowledge in religion, because knowledge is life and ignorance is death, and the Quran is light, life, and knowledge.

It was said: The meaning is when he calls you to Paradise (الجنة) because of the eternal life and the everlasting, perpetual blessing therein.

Each of these mentioned views accepts the application of the verse to it, except that the verse—as you have known—is absolute; there is no reason to divert it from its expansive meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَأَنَّهُ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ** — "and know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart and that toward Him you will be mustered." (8:24). "Intervention" (الحيلولة) is to come in between. The "heart" (القلب) is the well-known organ. It is used frequently in the Noble Quran for that by which the human being perceives and through which the judgments of his inner emotions appear, such as love, hatred, fear, hope, longing, anxiety, and the like. Thus, the heart is that which decides and judges, it is that which loves a thing and hates another, it is that which fears, hopes, longs, rejoices, and grieves. It is, in reality, the human soul (النفس) acting through the powers and inner emotions with which it has been equipped.

The human being—like other species Allah has originated which are parts of the world of creation—is composed of various parts, equipped with powers and tools subordinate to his existence, which he owns and employs for the purposes of his existence. All are linked to him with a link that makes the scattered parts and components, despite their multiplicity, and the diverse powers and tools, despite their number, a single complete whole that acts and refrains, moves and rests, in its unity and individuality.

However, since Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator of the human being and the Bringer into existence of every single part of his existence and his diverse powers and tools, He is the One who encompasses him and every single part of his existence and its subordinates. He owns each of them with the reality of the meaning of ownership (الملك), disposing of it as He wills, and He makes the human being own whatever He wills of them as He wills. Thus, He is the Intermediary (المتوسط) intervening between the human being and every part of the parts of his existence and every subordinate of the subordinates of his person: between him and his heart, between him and his hearing, between him and his sight, between him and his body, between him and his soul. He disposes of them by bringing them into existence, and He disposes of them by making the human being own whatever He wills of them as He wills, giving him what He gives and depriving him of what He deprives.

The like of the human being in this regard are all other existents. There is nothing in the cosmos that has an essence (ذات) and subordinates of essence—of powers, effects, and acts—except that Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner, in the true sense of the word, of its essence and the subordinates of its essence. He is the One who makes it own both its essence and the subordinates of its essence. Thus, He is the Intervening Intermediary between it and its essence, and between it and the subordinates of its essence—of its powers, effects, and acts.

So Allah, the Glorified, is the Intervening Intermediary between the human being and his heart and everything the human being owns and is linked and connected to with any kind of link and connection. He is closer to him than anything, as the Exalted said: **وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ** — "and We are nearer to him than [his] jugular vein" (50:16).

To this reality points His saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَأَنَّهُ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ**. For He, the Exalted, by virtue of being the Owner of everything—including the human being—with a real ownership such that there is no real owner besides Him, is closer to him even than himself and the powers of his soul which he owns, because He, the Glorified, is the One who makes him own them. Thus, He is an Intervening Intermediary between him and them, making him own them and linking them to him. Understand this.

Therefore, He followed the sentence with His saying: **وَأَنَّهُ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ**, for the Mustering (الحشر) and the Resurrection (البعث) is that at which it becomes clear that the true kingdom (الملك الحق) belongs to Allah alone without any partner, and at that time every formal ownership and apparent sovereignty will be nullified except His true kingdom, may His praise be exalted. As the Glorified said: **لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** — "‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’" (40:16), and He said: **يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ** — "the day when no soul will be of any avail to another soul and all command that day will belong to Allah." (82:19).

It is as if the verse is saying: Know that Allah is the real Owner of you and your hearts, and He is closer to you than anything, and that you will be mustered to Him, so the reality of His ownership of you and His sovereignty over you will appear on that day, and nothing will avail you against Him.

As for the connection of the discourse—namely the link of His saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ** etc., to His saying: **اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** — it is because His intervention, the Glorified, between a man and his heart cuts off the root of every excuse for not responding to Allah and the Messenger when he calls him to that which gives him life. This is monotheism (التوحيد), which is the reality of the true call. For when Allah, the Glorified, was closer to him than anything, even than his heart which he knows through his immediate consciousness (الوجدان) before anything else, He, the Exalted—alone without partner—is more known to him than his heart, which is the means of his perception and the cause of the origin of his recognition and knowledge.

Thus, he knows Allah as one God without partner before his knowledge of his heart and everything he knows through his heart. So whenever he doubts a thing or wavers in a matter, he will never doubt his one God who is the Lord of everything, and he will never go astray in identifying this true word.

So when the caller of truth calls him to the word of truth and the religion of monotheism which would give him life if he responded to it, it is incumbent upon him to respond to the caller of Allah. For he has no excuse in abandoning the response, justifying it by saying that he did not know the truth of what he was called to, or that it was confused for him, or that the schools of thought exhausted him in approaching the explicit truth. For Allah, the Glorified, is the explicit truth (الحق الصريح) whom no veil hides and no cover conceals, since every supposed veil—Allah, the Glorified, is closer than it to the human being. And every doubt or whisper that fluctuates in the heart—Allah, the Glorified, is an Intermediary coming in between it—along with the locus it has, which is the heart—and the human being. Thus, there is no way for the human being to be ignorant of Allah or to doubt His oneness.

Also, since Allah, the Glorified, is intervening between a man and his heart, He is closer to his heart than he is, just as He is closer to him than his heart. For the Intervening Intermediary is closer to each of the two sides than the other side is. If He, the Exalted, is closer to the heart of the human being than he is, then He is more knowing of what is in his heart than he is.

Therefore, it is upon the human being, when the caller of truth calls him to that which gives him life of the truth, to respond to his call with his heart just as he responds to it with his tongue, and not to conceal in his heart what does not agree with what he answered with his tongue—which is hypocrisy (النفاق). For Allah is more knowing of what is in his heart than he is, and he will be mustered to Him, so He will inform him of the reality of his deed and tell him of what he folded within his heart. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ** — "the day when they will emerge [from their graves]: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah." (40:16), and He said: **وَلا يَكْتُمُونَ اللهَ حَدِيثاً** — "and they will not be able to conceal any matter from Allah." (4:42).

Also, since Allah, the Glorified, is the One intervening between the human being and his heart, and He is the Owner of the heart in the real sense of ownership, He is the One who disposes of the heart before the human being, and He has the right to dispose of it as He wills. So whatever the human being finds in his heart of faith, doubt, fear, hope, tranquility, anxiety, agitation, or anything else attributed to him by choice or necessity—it has an attribution to Him, the Exalted, through His disposal in that which is closer to him than anything, a disposal by granting success (التوفيق) or abandonment (الخذلان) or any type of divine upbringing (التربية الإلهية). He disposes as He wills and judges as He intends, without any preventer preventing Him or any censure or blame threatening Him. As the Exalted said: **وَاللهُ يَحُكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ** — "Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment" (13:41), and the Exalted said: **لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — "To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise, and He has power over all things." (64:1).

Thus, it is out of ignorance for a human being to trust in what he finds in his heart of faith in the truth, or donning a good intention, or a resolution for good, or a concern for righteousness and godwariness—in the sense that he sees his independence in owning his heart and his absolute power over what he intends. For the heart is between the fingers of the All-Beneficent (الرحمن); He turns it as He wills, and He is its Owner in the real sense of ownership and encompasses it in the full sense of the word. The Exalted said: **وَنُقَلِّبُ أَفْئِدَتَهُمْ وَأَبْصارَهُمْ كَما لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** — "We shall turn their hearts and sights just as they did not believe in it the first time" (6:110). So it is obligatory upon him to believe in the truth and resolve upon good while being in fear of Allah the Exalted that He might turn him from happiness to wretchedness and change his heart from a state of uprightness to a state of inversion and swerving. He should not feel secure from the plot of Allah (مكر الله), for none feels secure from the plot of Allah except the people who are losers.

Likewise, if the human being finds his heart not approaching the word of truth and the resolution for good and righteous action, he must hasten to respond to Allah and the Messenger in what he calls him to of that which gives him life. He should not flee from the causes of despair and the factors of hopelessness that attack him from the side of his heart. For Allah, the Glorified, intervenes between a man and his heart, and He is capable of rectifying his inner secret and changing his heart to the best state and encompassing him with a spirit from Him and mercy. For the matter belongs only to Him, and He has said: **إِنَّهُ لا يَيْأَسُ مِنْ رَوْحِ اللهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْكافِرُونَ** — "no one despairs of Allah’s comfort except the faithless lot." (12:87), and He said: **وَمَنْ يَقْنَطُ مِنْ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّهِ إِلَّا الضَّالُّونَ** — "And who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?" (15:56).

The noble verse—as you see—is among the most comprehensive of Quranic verses. It contains a real recognition among the divine recognitions—the issue of intervention (الحيلولة). It cuts off the excuse of those who feign ignorance in the recognition of Allah, the Glorified, among the disbelievers and polytheists. It uproots the delusion of hypocrisy from its source by directing the souls of the hypocrites to the station of their Lord and that He is more knowing of what is in their hearts than they are. It delivers to the Muslims and those who are on the path of faith in Allah and His signs a psychological issue that teaches them that they are not independent in the ownership of their hearts, nor are they cut off in that from their Lord. Thereby, the vice of pride (الكبر) is removed from one who sees for himself independence and sovereignty in what he owns, so he is not deluded by what he witnesses of the heart's godwariness and the inner secret's faith. And the vice of despair and hopelessness is removed from one whose heart is surrounded by the calamities of desire and the inducements of worldly turning-away, such that he becomes sluggish in believing in the truth and approaching good, and that despair and hopelessness inherit him.

From what has preceded, it appears that His saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ** etc., is a justification for His saying, the Exalted: **اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** according to all the possibilities of its meaning.

By this, it also appears that the verse is broader in meaning than what the exegetes have provided in its interpretation: such as the saying of one who said: The meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, is closer to the man than his heart, similar to His saying: **وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ**, and in it is a severe warning.

And the saying of one who said: The meaning is that the heart cannot conceal any matter from Allah, for Allah is closer to the heart of the human being than himself; so what the human being knows from his heart, Allah knows it before him.

And the saying of one who said: The meaning is that He intervenes between a man and the benefit from his heart through death, so he cannot rectify what has passed; so hasten to acts of obedience before the intervention occurs and leave procrastination. In it is an exhortation toward obedience before the arrival of the preventer.

And the saying of one who said: Its meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, owns the turning of hearts from state to state. It is as if they feared the fighting, so Allah, the Glorified, informed them that He would change their fear into security by intervening between them and what they were thinking of regarding the causes of fear.

It has been reported in the hadith from the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام) that what is meant by this is that Allah, the Glorified, intervenes between the human being and his knowing that the truth is falsehood or that falsehood is truth. This will come in the "Discussion of Narrations," Allah the Exalted willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ** — "And beware of a punishment which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively, and know that Allah is severe in retribution." (8:25). Ali and al-Baqir (عليه السلام) from the Imams of the People of the House, as well as Zayd ibn Thabit, al-Rabi‘ ibn Anas, and Abu al-Aliyah, read it—according to what is in *al-Majma‘*—as *latasibanna* (لتصيبن) with the "lam" and the heavy "nun" of emphasis. The famous reading is *la tasibanna* (لا تصيبن) with the prohibitive "la" and the heavy "nun" of emphasis.

On any estimation, the verse warns all the believers against a trial (فتنة) — *a test or tribulation that may lead to discord or deviation* — that is specific to the wrongdoers (الظالمين) among them and does not extend beyond them to others among the disbelievers and polytheists. Its specificity to the wrongdoers among the believers, while commanding the generality of them to beware of it, indicates that although it is carried out by some of the community, its evil effect encompasses everyone. Then His saying, the Exalted: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ** — "and know that Allah is severe in retribution" is a threat to everyone with severe punishment. There is no evidence to indicate the restriction of this punishment to the worldly life or its being of the worldly torment, such as ethnic disputes, the prevalence of killing and corruption, and the loss of security and peace, and the like.

The implication of this is that the mentioned trial—despite its specificity to some of the people—is among those things that make it obligatory for the generality of the nation (الأمة) to hasten to repel it, cut off its root, and extinguish the flame of its fire through what Allah has made obligatory upon them of forbidding the wrong (النهي عن المنكر) and commanding the right (الأمر بالمعروف).

Thus, the meaning of the discourse boils down to a warning to the generality of Muslims against negligence in the matter of internal disputes that threaten their unity and cause the splitting of their staff and the discord of their word. It will not be long before it divides them into factions and breaks them into parts, and the kingdom will belong to whoever overcomes among them, and victory will be for the word of corruption, not for the word of truth and the upright religion (الدين الحنيف) in which the generality of Muslims share.

So this is a trial that is carried out by some of them specifically—and they are the wrongdoers—except that its evil effect encompasses all and includes everyone. Thus, humiliation and wretchedness, and all that is expected of bitter calamity through the emergence of dispute among them, will absorb them. They are all responsible before Allah, and Allah is severe in retribution.

Allah the Exalted has left the matter of this trial vague and has not identified it by its full name and description. However, His saying afterward: **لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً** and His saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ** —as preceded—clarifies it somewhat. This is that it is the dispute of some of the nation with another part of it in a matter where the direction of truth is known to all of it, yet some persist in refusing to accept the truth and proceed toward the wrong through their wrongdoing. Their help in their wrongdoing is not withheld, nor are they forbidden from the wrong they commit. It is not just any wrongdoing, but the wrongdoing whose evil effect spreads to all the believers and the generality of the nation because of His command to everyone to beware of it. So the wrongdoing that belongs to some of the nation and which everyone must beware of is nothing but that which is of the type of overcoming the true Islamic government and the manifestation of destroying the definitive matters (القطعيات) of the Book and the Sunnah which are among its rights.

Whatever it may be, among the trials that occurred in the early days of Islam is that to which the verse applies most clearly. Through them, religious unity was destroyed, division appeared, strength was exhausted, and power vanished, along with what they contained of killing, captivity, looting, violation of honors and sanctities, abandonment of the Book, and cancellation of the Sunnah. The Messenger said: "O my Lord! Indeed my people have taken this Quran as something to be abandoned."

Among the encompassing nature of its ill-omen and the deep-rootedness of its corruption is that the nation is unable to emerge from its painful torment even after they become aware of their evil deeds and their neglect toward Allah. Whenever they want to emerge from it, they are returned into it, and "taste the punishment of the burning."

Some exegetes have perceived that the verse warns the nation and threatens them with a trial that encompasses their generality, divides their gathering, scatters their ranks, and promises them the severe punishment of Allah. This perception was good, except that he labored in directing the punishment toward worldly punishment and struggled in restricting the absolute nature of retribution in the verse. How can they reach it from a far-off place?

Let us return to the wording of the verse:

As for the reading of the People of the House (عليه السلام) and Zayd: "And beware of a punishment which shall certainly visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively," the "lam" in *latasibanna* is for an oath and the heavy "nun" is for its emphasis. The estimation is: "And beware of a punishment, I swear it shall certainly visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively." The word "exclusively" (خاصة) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the trial. The meaning is: Beware of a trial whose visitation is specific to those who did wrong among you, O you who are addressed, and they are those who believed. You must remember what has already been explained: that the expression "those who have faith" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) in the Quran is an honorific address for the believers at the beginning of the mission and the start of the spread of the call, unless there is a context diverting from that. Then remember that the trials of the early days of Islam end with the companions of Badr. The verse, in any case, commands everyone to beware of a trial that some of them stir up, and this is only because its evil effect encompasses everyone, as preceded.

As for the famous reading: **وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً**, they have mentioned: that the "la" in *la tasibanna* is prohibitive and the "nun" is for the emphasis of the prohibition. *La tasibanna* is not an answer to the command in "beware" (اتَّقُوا); rather, the speech proceeds in the manner of a beginning and a resumption (استيناف), like His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّمْلُ ادْخُلُوا مَساكِنَكُمْ لا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمانُ وَجُنُودُهُ** — "O ants, enter your dwellings, lest Solomon and his hosts crush you" (27:18). For He said first: "And beware of a punishment," then He resumed and said: "it shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively," because of the connection of the two sentences in meaning.

Some grammarians have perhaps allowed *la tasibanna* to be a prohibition occurring in the answer to the command, as it is said: "Beware of Zayd, let him not strike you" or "let him certainly not strike you." The estimation is: "Beware of Zayd, for if you beware of him, he will not strike you." It was not stipulated for the "nun" of emphasis that it should not enter the predicate (الخبر).

Some have perhaps said: that "la" is extra (زائدة) and the meaning is: "Beware of a trial that shall certainly visit..." the verse.

Others have perhaps mentioned: "that the origin of *la tasibanna* (لا تُصِيبَنَ) is *latasibanna* (لتصيبن); the fatha of the 'lam' was saturated until the 'alif' was generated." The saturation of the fatha is not rare in poetry. He said:

*So you are from the calamities when you throw*
*And from the blame of men in a place of distance (بمنتزاح)*

He means *bimuntazahi* (بمنتزح). Both views are far-fetched; His speech, the Exalted, is not to be interpreted upon the like of them.

The outcome of the meaning according to this view—namely according to the reading *la tasibanna*—also leads to what the first reading *latasibanna* provides, as you have known.

The verse—as you have come to know—contains a social discourse (خطاب اجتماعي) addressed to the collective body of the nation (الأمة). This supports the view that the discourse in the preceding verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life" (8:24), is also a social discourse addressed to the entirety of the believers. It follows from this that the intended meaning of the "summons to that which will give them life" is the call to agree upon holding fast to the rope of Allah, establishing the religion, and avoiding division therein, as He said: **وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعاً وَلا تَفَرَّقُوا** "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided" (3:103); and He said: **أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** "Maintain the religion, and do not be divided in it" (42:13); and His saying: **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** "This is indeed My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way" (6:153).

Through this, some of the previously mentioned interpretations regarding His saying: **إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** "when he summons you to that which will give you life" are supported, as is the case with His saying: **أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ** "that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart" (8:24). The verse is specific to this meaning according to the context (السياق), even if it yields a broader meaning when taken in itself, independent of the context. The critical researcher (الباحث الناقد) will not find it difficult to distinguish this, and Allah is the Guide.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ أَنْتُمْ قَلِيلٌ مُسْتَضْعَفُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ تَخافُونَ أَنْ يَتَخطَّفَكُمُ النَّاسُ** "Remember when you were few, abased in the land, fearing that the people would snatch you away..." (8:26) to the end of the verse. **Abasement (الاستضعاف)** — *the act of deeming something weak by undermining its status*; **snatching (التخطف والخطف والاختطاف)** — *taking something with a swift seizure*; **sheltering (الإيواء)** — *providing a person with a refuge and a dwelling to which they return and seek shelter*; and **strengthening (التأييد)** is derived from *al-ayd*, which means strength (القوة).

The context indicates that the intended meaning of His saying: **إِذْ أَنْتُمْ قَلِيلٌ مُسْتَضْعَفُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ** "when you were few, abased in the land" refers to the time when the Muslims were besieged in Mecca before the migration (الهجرة), while they were few and deemed weak. By His saying: **تَخافُونَ أَنْ يَتَخَطَّفَكُمُ النَّاسُ** "fearing that the people would snatch you away," He refers to the polytheists of the Arabs and the chieftains of Quraysh. By His saying: **فَآواكُمْ** "then He gave you shelter," He means in Medina. By His saying: **وَأَيَّدَكُمْ بِنَصْرِهِ** "and strengthened you with His help," He refers to the favor of victory He bestowed upon them at Badr. By His saying: **وَرَزَقَكُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ** "and provided you with good things," He refers to the spoils of war (الغنائم) that He provided for them and made lawful.

Although the conditions of the believers and His favors upon them regarding shelter mentioned in the verse are specific to the Emigrants (المهاجرين) among them rather than the Helpers (الأنصار), the intent is to bestow favor upon all of them—both Emigrants and Helpers—for they are a single nation (أمة واحدة) united by one religion. Furthermore, the favors Allah mentioned in the verse, such as strengthening with victory and providing from the good things, encompass everyone. This is according to what the verse necessitates by virtue of its occurrence within the context of the verses of Badr. However, taken alone and by itself, it encompasses all Muslims insofar as they are a single nation whose later generations trace back to their predecessors. The manifestation of Islam began among them while they were few and abased in Mecca, fearing that people would snatch them away; then He sheltered them in Medina, increased their numbers through the Helpers, strengthened them with His victory at Badr and elsewhere, and provided them with all good things—spoils and other blessings—so that they might be grateful.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** "O you who have faith! Do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts knowingly" (8:27) to the end of the two verses. **Betrayal (الخيانة)** — *the violation of a trust (الأمانة), which is the preservation of security for a right among rights through a covenant, a will, or the like*. Al-Raghib said: "**Betrayal (الخيانة)** and **hypocrisy (النفاق)** are one and the same, except that betrayal is used in consideration of the covenant and trust, while hypocrisy is used in consideration of religion; then they overlap. Thus, betrayal is the opposition to truth by breaking a covenant in secret. The opposite of betrayal is **trustworthiness (الأمانة)**. It is said: 'I betrayed so-and-so' or 'I betrayed the trust of so-and-so.' Based on this is His saying: **لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ** (Do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts)." End quote.

His saying: **وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ** "and [do not] betray your trusts" may be in the jussive mood (مجزوماً), coordinated with the preceding "do not betray" (لا تخونوا), meaning: "and do not betray your trusts." It may also be in the subjunctive mood (منصوباً) due to the omission of the particle *an* (أن), the implied meaning being: "and [lest] you betray your trusts." The second view is supported by His subsequent saying: **وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** "knowingly."

This is because betrayal, although the prohibitory ruling (النهي التحريمي) only attaches to it upon knowledge (العلم)—for there is no prohibition without knowledge of the subject matter, nor any sanctity—knowledge is nonetheless among the general conditions (الشرائط العامة) without which no masterly obligation (تكليف مولوي) is actualized. Thus, there is no apparent subtle point (نكتة) in restricting the prohibition of betrayal to knowledge, since knowledge, being a general condition, does not require explicit mention. The apparent meaning of His saying: **وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** "while you know"—with the object of the verb omitted—is: "while you have knowledge that it is a betrayal." It is not as some have said: "while you know the corruptions of betrayal, its evil consequences, and Allah's prohibition of it," for there is no evidence for that from the perspective of the wording or the context.

The correct view is that the sentence implies: "and [lest] you betray your trusts," and the entirety of His saying: **لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ** constitutes a single prohibition concerning a specific type of betrayal, which is the betrayal of the trust of Allah and His Messenger, which is itself a betrayal of the trust of the believers themselves. For among trusts, some are the trust of Allah (أمانة الله) with the people, such as His legislated rulings; some are the trust of the Messenger (أمانة الرسول), such as his noble conduct (سيرته الحسنة); and some are the trusts of the people with one another, such as their wealth or their secrets. There are also trusts shared by Allah, His Messenger, and the believers, which are the matters commanded by Allah, implemented by the Messenger, and which benefit the people and form the backbone of their society—such as political secrets and military objectives, the disclosure of which would ruin the hopes of the religion and lead the efforts of the Islamic government astray, thereby nullifying the right of Allah and His Messenger and resulting in harm to the generality of the believers.

This type of trust—its betrayal is a betrayal of Allah, the Messenger, and the believers. The betrayer among the believers who commits this act betrays Allah and the Messenger while knowing that this trust he is betraying is a trust for himself and for the rest of his believing brothers; thus, he is betraying his own trust. No rational person would intentionally betray his own trust, for a human being, through the intellect (العقل) bestowed upon him, perceives the ugliness of betraying a trust—so how could he betray his own trust?

Therefore, the meaning of His saying: **وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** —and Allah knows best—is: "and you betray, within the betrayal of Allah and the Messenger, your own trusts, while the case is that you know they are your own trusts yet you betray them." What rational person would proceed to betray his own trust and cause harm that only returns to his own person? Thus, concluding the prohibition with His saying: **وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** "knowingly" is to stir righteous zeal (العصبية الحقة) and to provoke the judgment of the primordial nature (الفطرة), not to state one of the conditions of religious obligation (التكليف).

It is as if some individuals among the Muslims were disclosing matters of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) hidden determinations from the polytheists or informing them of some of his secrets; so Allah called it betrayal and forbade it, counting it as a betrayal of Allah, the Messenger, and the believers.

This is supported by His saying after this prohibition: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ** "Know that your possessions and your children are only a test..." (8:28) etc. The apparent context is that it is connected to what preceded it and not independent of it. It implies then that His exhorting them regarding wealth and children alongside the prohibition of betraying Allah, the Messenger, and their trusts was because the one among them who informed the polytheists of the Messenger of Allah's hidden secrets did so to gain favor with them, fearing they might transgress against his wealth and children whom he had left in Mecca upon migrating to Medina. Thus, they began informing them of news to establish affection and preserve wealth and children, or something similar, akin to what occurred with Abu Lubabah regarding the Banu Qurayzah.

This supports what has been narrated concerning the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول): that Abu Sufyan left Mecca with much wealth, so Gabriel informed the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of his departure and advised him to go out against him and keep the matter secret. However, one of them wrote to him with the news, so Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ**. There are other narrations regarding the revelation of the verse that will come, Allah willing, in the following traditional discussion.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقاناً وَيُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَاللهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ** "O you who have faith! If you are God-conscious, He will grant you a criterion, and absolve you of your misdeeds, and forgive you, and Allah is the Lord of great bounty" (8:29). **The Criterion (الفرقان)** — *that by which one distinguishes between one thing and another*. In the verse, by the evidence of the context and its derivation from godwariness (التقوى), it is the criterion between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), whether that be in belief—by distinguishing between faith and disbelief, and every guidance and misguidance—or in action—by distinguishing between obedience and disobedience, and everything that pleases Allah or angers Him—or in opinion and insight—by separating between correctness and error. All of this is what the tree of godwariness produces. The term "Criterion" is used unconditionally in the verse and not restricted, as the previous verses have enumerated various types of good and evil, and all of them require a criterion.

Similar to this verse in meaning is His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَتَّقِ اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَحْتَسِبُ وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ** "And whoever is God-conscious, He will make for him a way out, and provide for him from whence he does not reckon. And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him" (65:2-3). The discussion regarding the meaning of the "absolving of misdeeds" and "forgiveness" has already passed. The verse serves as a summary of the discourse regarding the commands and prohibitions contained in the preceding verses; that is: if you are God-conscious, that which pleases Allah in all that has passed will not be confused for you with that which angers Him, and He will absolve you of your misdeeds and forgive you, and Allah is the Lord of great bounty.

## (Commentary)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from 'Aqil al-Khuza'i: that the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) said: "Indeed, terror and fear of the struggle (الجهاد) against those deserving of struggle—and those who cooperate in misguidance—is misguidance in religion and a stripping away of the worldly life along with humiliation and belittlement. In it is the deserving of the Fire by fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف) at the presence of combat. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا زَحْفاً فَلا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبارَ** (O you who have faith! When you encounter the faithless on the battlefield, do not turn your backs to them)."

In *al-Faqih* and *al-'Ilal*, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Shadhan: that Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) wrote to him among the answers to his questions: "Allah has forbidden fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف) because of the weakness it causes in the religion, the belittling of the Messengers and the just Imams, the abandonment of aiding them against enemies, and the punishment for them for abandoning what they were called to—of acknowledging the Lordship, manifesting justice, abandoning oppression, and putting an end to corruption—due to the resulting boldness of the enemy against the Muslims, and the ensuing captivity, killing, nullification of the religion of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and other forms of corruption."

**I say:** The narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) are exhaustive (مستفيضة) that fleeing from the battlefield is among the major destructive sins (المعاصي الكبيرة الموبقة). A portion of this has already passed in the discussion on major sins in the exegesis of His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ تَجْنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31) in the fourth volume of this book.

There are also narrations to this effect from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah, as in the two *Sahihs* of al-Bukhari and Muslim, from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: **اجتنبوا السبع الموبقات** — "Avoid the seven destructive sins." They asked: "And what are they, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: **الشرك بالله وقتل النفس التي حرم الله إلا بالحق والسحر وأكل الربا وأكل مال اليتيم والتولي يوم الزحف وقذف المحصنات الغافلات المؤمنات** — "Associating partners with Allah; killing the soul which Allah has forbidden except by right; sorcery; consuming usury; consuming the property of the orphan; turning away on the day of the battlefield; and slandering chaste, oblivious, believing women." There are other narrations from Ibn 'Abbas and others indicating that fleeing from the battlefield is among the major sins.

Yes, His saying, the Exalted: **الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللهُ عَنْكُمْ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً فَإِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ مِائَةٌ صابِرَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا مِائَتَيْنِ** "Now Allah has lightened your burden, and He knows that there is a weakness in you. So if there be a hundred of you, patient, they shall overcome two hundred..." (8:66) restricts the absolute nature of the verse prohibiting fleeing to cases where the ratio is less than three to one.

It has been narrated through their paths from 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar, Ibn 'Abbas, Abu Hurayrah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and others, as in *al-Durr al-Manthur*: that the prohibition of fleeing from the battlefield in this verse was specific to the day of Badr.

This has sometimes been justified by the claim that the verse was revealed on the day of Badr, and that the adverbial phrase in His saying: **وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ** "and whoever turns his back to them on that day" (8:16) refers to the day of Badr. However, you have already seen that the context of the verses testifies to their revelation after the day of Badr, and that the intended meaning of "on that day" (يومئذ) is the day of the battlefield (يوم الزحف), not specifically the day of Badr. Furthermore, even if we were to assume its revelation on the day of Badr, the specificity of the occasion (خصوص السبب) does not affect the generality of the verse's significance (عموم المدلول), as is the case with all other verses that combine general significance with a specific occasion.
