The author of *al-Manar* said in his exegesis: "Basing the specification on the circumstantial evidence (قرينة الحال) might only be plausible if the verse had been revealed before the engagement of combat—contrary to the majority view—given the unique characteristics of the Battle of Badr, such as its being the first battle in Islam; had the Muslims been defeated while the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among them, the tribulation (الفتنة) would have been immense. Also, the strengthening of the Muslims with angels to make them firm, and His promise to grant them victory and cast terror into the hearts of their enemies.

So, if we look at the collective characteristics and the circumstantial evidence in the prohibition, the view that the prohibition coupled with the severe threat in the verse was specific to it becomes plausible. Add to this that Allah, the Exalted, tested the Companions (رض) with turning away and retreating in battle twice while he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was with them: on the day of Uhud, regarding which Allah, the Exalted, says: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا مِنْكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ إِنَّمَا اسْتَزَلَّهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ بِبَعْضِ ما كَسَبُوا وَلَقَدْ عَفَا اللهُ عَنْهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ** 'Those of you who turned back on the day when the two hosts met, it was only Satan who made them slip because of some of what they had earned. But Allah has certainly excused them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing' (3:155); and on the day of Hunayn, regarding which Allah, the Exalted, says: **لَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللهُ فِي مَواطِنَ كَثِيرَةٍ وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنْكُمْ شَيْئاً وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** 'Allah has certainly helped you in many situations, and on the day of Hunayn, when your great number impressed you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth became narrow for you despite its expanse; then you turned back [and] fled. Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the believers...' (9:25-26). This does not negate that turning away is forbidden and among the major sins, nor does it necessitate that every instance of turning away for reasons other than the two exceptions in the verse of *al-Anfal* causes its perpetrator to incur a great wrath from Allah and have his abode in Hell—an evil destination. Rather, it may be less than that, and it is restricted by the verse of the license for weakness (رخصة الضعف) coming later in this Surah, and by the prohibition of throwing oneself into destruction (التهلكة) in its generality, as previously discussed in Surah al-Baqarah and as will be detailed shortly.

Ahmad and the authors of the *Sunan*, except al-Nasa'i, narrated from the hadith of Ibn 'Umar who said: 'I was in one of the detachments of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and the people fled a flight, and I was among those who fled—so we said: "What shall we do—having fled from the battlefield and incurred the wrath?" Then we said: "What if we entered Medina and stayed the night?" Then we said: "What if we presented ourselves to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)? If there is repentance for us, [well and good], otherwise we shall depart." So we came to him before the morning prayer—and he came out and said: "Who are the fleers (الفرارون)?" We said: "We are the fleers." He said: "Nay, you are the returners to the fight (العكارون); I am your party (فئتكم) and the party of the Muslims." He said: "So we came until we kissed his hand."'

And the wording of Abu Dawud: 'So we said: "Let us enter Medina and stay the night therein—so we may go and no one sees us." So we entered and said: "What if we presented ourselves to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—if there is repentance for us, we shall stay—and if it is otherwise, we shall depart." So we sat for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before the dawn prayer—and when he came out, we stood up to him and said: "We are the fleers..."' etc.

Some have interpreted this hadith with an expansion in the meaning of 'withdrawing to a party' (التحيز إلى فئة) such that no meaning remains for the threat, nor any rule for the language. Al-Tirmidhi said regarding it: 'It is fair (حسن); we do not know it except from the hadith of Yazid ibn Abi Ziyad.' I say: He is disputed; many have weakened him. Ibn Hibban said: 'He was truthful, except that when he grew old, his memory failed and he changed, so fabrications occurred in his hadith; so whoever heard from him before the change, his hearing is sound.' The summary of the matter is: this hadith has no weight in this issue, neither in its text (متناً) nor its chain (سنداً), and in its meaning is a report from 'Umar which is even less than it, so it is not placed in the scales of this issue." End quote.

**I say:** That which he cited at the beginning of his speech regarding the reasons and circumstantial evidence surrounding the Battle of Badr—such as its being the first battle in Islam, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being among them, and the like—are shared in terms of the reality of the underlying rationale (الملاك) between it and battles like Uhud, al-Khandaq, Khaybar, and Hunayn. Islam in those days was in dire need of fighting men and their steadfastness in battlefields, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among them, and Allah promised them victory and sent down angels in some of them to strengthen them and cast terror into the hearts of their enemies.

As for the verses he mentioned that were revealed concerning their flight on the day of Uhud and the day of Hunayn, there is no indication in them that the threat of the verse of *al-Anfal* did not encompass them at that time. What barrier prevents that when the verse is absolute (مطلقة) and there is no restrictor (مقيد) to limit it?

It is strange that he admits their flight on those two days was a forbidden major sin, yet then says: "this does not necessitate that it is among those things whose perpetrator incurs wrath from Allah and whose abode is Hell—an evil destination; rather, it may be less than that," despite the fact that the destructive major sins (الكبائر الموبقة) are those acts of disobedience for which Allah has threatened the Fire.

Even stranger is his saying: "it is restricted by the verse of the license for weakness coming later in this Surah, and by the prohibition of throwing oneself into destruction in its generality!" For the verse of the license for weakness only indicates the permission to flee if the number of the enemy's advancing forces exceeds double.

And the verse prohibiting throwing oneself into destruction—if it were to indicate by its generality more than what the verse of the license for weakness indicates, the verse of *al-Anfal* would become redundant and remain without an instance of application (مصداق). Similarly, the interpretation of His saying: **أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ** "or withdrawing to a party" according to what the narration of Ibn 'Umar necessitates would lead to the nullification of the verse, as mentioned by the author of *al-Manar*. Thus, it is summarized that there is no escape from maintaining the verse upon its apparent absolute sense.

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from Musa ibn Ja'far (عليه‌السلام): regarding the verse: **إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ** "unless it be to maneuver for battle," he said: "Moving away while intending to return to the attack against them." **أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ** "or withdrawing to a party," he said: "Meaning retreating to his companions without being defeated (هزيمة); whoever is defeated such that he passes the rank of his companions has indeed incurred wrath from Allah."

**I say:** The narration points to an important subtle point in the wording of the verse, which is that the prohibition in the verse is attached to "turning the backs" (تولي الأدبار), which is more general than "defeat" (الانهزام). So when the two cases are excepted—namely maneuvering for battle and withdrawing to a party, which are not cases of fleeing due to defeat—the cases of defeat remain under the prohibition. Thus, every defeat before the enemies of the religion, if they do not exceed double in number, is forbidden and prohibited.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Shahr Ashub from al-Tha'labi from Dahhak from 'Ikrimah from Ibn 'Abbas: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ** "And you did not throw when you threw" (8:17), that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to 'Ali: "Hand me a handful of pebbles." He handed them to him, and he threw them into the faces of Quraysh; there remained no one but that his eyes were filled with pebbles.

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn 'Abbas. Al-'Ayyashi narrated in his *Tafsir* the hadith of the handing over from Muhammad ibn Kulayb al-Asadi from his father from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), and in another report from 'Ali (عليه‌السلام).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir extracted from Muhammad ibn Qays and Muhammad ibn Ka'b (رضي الله عنهما) who both said: "When the people drew near to one another, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took a handful of dust and threw it into the faces of the people, saying: **شاهت الوجوه** — 'May the faces be deformed.' It entered the eyes of all of them, and the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) advanced, killing them. Their defeat was due to the throw of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). So Allah revealed: **وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ** to His saying **سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ**."

**I say:** The intent behind the "revelation of the verse" is its revelation after that, as it recounts the story, not its revelation at that exact moment; this is common in the occasions of revelation. Ibn Hisham mentioned in his *Sirah*: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) threw dust at them and then ordered his companions to charge, and thus was the defeat.

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, Ibn Mandah, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* extracted from Ibn Shihab from 'Abdullah ibn Tha'labah ibn Su'ayr: that Abu Jahl said when the two groups met: "O Allah, [destroy] the one among us who has most severed the ties of kinship and brought us that which we do not recognize; so destroy him this morning." That was a request for a decision (استفتاحاً) from him; so there was revealed: **إِنْ تَسْتَفْتِحُوا فَقَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْفَتْحُ** "If you seek a verdict, the verdict has certainly come to you" (8:19).

In *al-Majma'*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللهِ** "Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight..." (8:22). He said: al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) said: "They are the Banu 'Abd al-Dar; none of them had embraced Islam except Mus'ab ibn 'Umayr and an ally of theirs called Suwaybit."

In *Jami' al-Jawami'*: al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) said: "They are the Banu 'Abd al-Dar; none of them embraced Islam except Mus'ab ibn 'Umayr and Suwayd ibn Harmalah. They used to say: 'We are deaf, dumb, and blind to what Muhammad has brought.' They were all killed at Uhud, and they were the standard-bearers."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* something to this effect through paths from Ibn 'Abbas and Qatadah. The narration is of the nature of application (الجري والانطباق), and the verse is general.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ**. He said: "He said: Life (الحياة) is Paradise."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Abu al-Rabi' al-Shami who said: I asked Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ**. He said: "It was revealed concerning the guardianship (ولاية) of 'Ali (عليه‌السلام)."

**I say:** It was narrated in *Tafsir al-Burhan* from Ibn Mardawayh from his men, raised to Imam Muhammad ibn 'Ali al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام), and likewise from Abu al-Jarud from him (عليه‌السلام) as narrated by al-Qummi in his *Tafsir*. The narration is of the nature of application, as is the narration preceding it. We have already stated in the discussion on the verse that it is general.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, from Abu al-Jarud from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام): regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ** "and know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart." He says: "Between a man and his disobedience, lest it lead him to the Fire; and He intervenes between the disbeliever and his obedience, lest he complete his faith through it. And know that deeds are judged by their endings."

In *al-Mahasin*, with its chain of transmission from 'Ali ibn al-Hakam from Hisham ibn Salim from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام): regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ**. He said: "He intervenes between him and his knowing that falsehood is truth."

**I say:** Al-Saduq narrated it in *al-Ma'ani* from Ibn Abi 'Umayr from Hisham ibn Salim from him (عليه‌السلام).

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from Yunus ibn 'Ammar from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "The heart never becomes certain that truth is falsehood, nor does it ever become certain that falsehood is truth."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn 'Abbas (رضي الله عنهما) who said: I asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about this verse: **يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ**. He said: "He intervenes between the believer and disbelief, and He intervenes between the disbeliever and guidance."

**I say:** This is close to the report mentioned previously from Abu al-Jarud from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) regarding the meaning of the verse.

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from Hamzah al-Tayyar from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام): **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ**. He said: "It is that he desires a thing with his hearing, his sight, his tongue, and his hand—yet he does not commit any of it even if he desires it—for he does not approach it except that his heart is disapproving, not accepting what he approaches; he knows that truth is not in it."

**I say:** Al-Barqi narrated it in *al-Mahasin* with his chain from Hamzah al-Tayyar from him (عليه‌السلام). Al-'Ayyashi narrated something close to it in his *Tafsir* from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام). The meaning of the narration goes back to the two narrations mentioned previously from Hisham ibn Salim and Yunus ibn 'Ammar from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام).

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Sayqal: Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) was asked about **وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً** "And beware of a punishment which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively" (8:25). He said: "I was informed that they are the People of the Camel (أصحاب الجمل)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, he said: He said: "It was revealed concerning Talhah and al-Zubayr—when they fought the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) and wronged him."

In *al-Majma'*, from al-Hakim with his chain from Qatadah from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib from Ibn 'Abbas who said: When this verse was revealed: **وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً**, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Whoever wrongs 'Ali regarding this position of mine after my death—it is as if he has denied my prophethood and the prophethood of the prophets before me."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Nu'aym ibn Hammad in *al-Fitan*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from al-Zubayr (رضي الله عنه) who said: "We recited it for a long time and did not think we were among its people—then suddenly we were the ones intended by it: **وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً**."

In it, Ibn Jarir and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from al-Suddi regarding the verse: "This was revealed concerning the people of Badr specifically—so it struck them on the Day of the Camel and they fought one another—and among those killed were Talhah and al-Zubayr, and they were from the people of Badr."

In it, Ahmad, al-Bazzar, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn 'Asakir extracted from Mutarrif who said: We said to al-Zubayr: "O Abu 'Abdullah, you neglected the Caliph until he was killed—then you came seeking his blood?" Al-Zubayr (رضي الله عنه) said: "We used to recite during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), Abu Bakr, 'Umar, and 'Uthman (رضي الله عنهم): **وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً**—and we did not think we were its people—until it fell upon us where it fell."

In it, 'Abd ibn Humayd and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Qatadah (رضي الله عنه) regarding the verse: "By Allah, the possessors of intellect among the companions of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) knew that there would be tribulations (فتن)."

In it, Abu al-Shaykh, Abu Nu'aym, and al-Daylami in *Musnad al-Firdawsi* extracted from Ibn 'Abbas (رضي الله عنهما) from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): regarding His saying: **وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ أَنْتُمْ قَلِيلٌ مُسْتَضْعَفُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ تَخافُونَ أَنْ يَتَخَطَّفَكُمُ النَّاسُ**. It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, and who are 'the people'?" He said: "The people of Persia."

**I say:** The narration does not harmonize with the context of the verse.

In it: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ**. Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Jabir ibn 'Abdullah (رضي الله عنه): that Abu Sufyan left Mecca—so Gabriel came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "Indeed Abu Sufyan is in such-and-such a place—so go out against him and keep it secret." But a man from the hypocrites wrote to Abu Sufyan: "Indeed Muhammad intends [to attack] you, so take your precaution." So Allah revealed: **لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ**.

**I say:** The meaning of the narration is close in application to what we derived from the verse in the preceding commentary.

In it, Ibn Jarir extracted from al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah who said: "This verse was revealed concerning the killing of 'Uthman (رضي الله عنه)."

**I say:** The verse does not apply to it by its context at all.

In *al-Majma'*, from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), al-Kalbi, and al-Zuhri: It was revealed concerning Abu Lubabah ibn 'Abd al-Mundhir al-Ansari. That was when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) besieged the Jews of Qurayzah for twenty-one nights. They asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for a peace treaty—upon what he had made peace with their brothers from the Banu al-Nadir—that they should travel to their brothers in Adhri'at and Arihat in the land of Syria. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) refused to grant them that—unless they submitted to the judgment of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh. They said: "Send Abu Lubabah to us"—and he was a sincere advisor to them because his family, wealth, and children were with them. So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent him. He came to them and they said: "What do you see, O Abu Lubabah? Should we submit to the judgment of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh?" Abu Lubabah pointed with his hand to his throat: [meaning] that it is slaughter—so do not do it. Then Gabriel came to him and informed him of that.

Abu Lubabah said: "By Allah, my feet did not move from their place until I knew that I had betrayed Allah and His Messenger." So the verse was revealed concerning him. When it was revealed, he tied himself to one of the pillars of the mosque and said: "By Allah, I shall not taste food nor drink until I die or Allah turns to me in forgiveness." He remained for seven days without tasting food or drink until he fell unconscious. Then Allah turned to him in forgiveness. It was said to him: "O Abu Lubabah, you have been forgiven." He said: "No, by Allah, I shall not untie myself until the Messenger of Allah is the one who unties me." So he came to him and untied him with his hand.

Then Abu Lubabah said: "Indeed, part of the completion of my repentance is that I should leave the house of my people in which I committed the sin, and that I should divest myself of my wealth." The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "One-third is sufficient for you to give in charity."

**I say:** The story of Abu Lubabah and his repentance is authentic and capable of being applied to the content of the two verses, except that it occurred long after the story of Badr. The apparent sense of the two verses, when considered and compared to the verses preceding them, is that all are in a single context revealed shortly after the event of Badr. And Allah knows best.
